subject: AP Physics 2: Algebra-Based
text:
The figure above represents part of a student’s experiment in
which polar molecules are moving toward the top of the page
at high speed. The molecules are modeled as atoms with a
positive and a negative charge connected by a molecular
bond. The molecules pass through a narrow region of uniform
electric field directed to the right. Since the molecules have
no net charge, the student expects that the field will have no
effect on them and is puzzled to find that the molecules have
a higher average energy when they emerge on the other side
of the field. Which of the following could explain the
student’s observation? Select two answers.

options.A:
Since some molecules enter the field aligned at an angle
to the field—like the molecule on the left—the field
exerts a torque on them, which causes them to rotate
and gives them rotational kinetic energy.
options.B:
Since the molecules are polar, they behave as if they have
a net charge. The field exerts a net force on them that
does work and gives them linear kinetic energy.
options.C:
Since some molecules enter the field aligned parallel to
its direction—like the molecule in the middle—the field
exerts forces in opposite directions on the two atoms,
which stretches the molecular bonds and gives the
molecules internal energy.
options.D:
Since some molecules enter the field aligned
perpendicular to its direction—like the molecule on the
right—the field exerts forces in opposite directions on
the two atoms. The force on the positive charge is
greater, so the net force does work and gives them
linear kinetic energy.

subject: AP Physics 2: Algebra-Based
text:
Questions 29-31 refer to the following material.
Cosmic rays bombard Earth's upper atmosphere, creating subatomic particles called muons, which then stream toward the surface of Earth at nearly the speed of light. The muon is not a nucleon. It is an unstable fundamental particle with most properties the same as those of an electron but with a larger mass. In a frame of reference at rest with respect to the muon, the muon decays with a half-life of approximately $2.2\times10^{-6}\,\mathrm{s}$.

29. The number of muons that reach Earth's surface without decaying is higher than would be expected from the known half-life for muon decay. The measured discrepancy arises because the muons are moving close to the speed of light. Which of the following is a consequence of the high speed of the muons and best explains the measured discrepancy?

options.A:
Time intervals as measured in Earth’s rest
frame are different from time intervals
measured in the rest frame of a muon, so
more muons reach Earth’s surface.
options.B:
The size of a muon as measured on Earth is
different from the size measured in the rest
frame of a muon, so it is easier for a muon
to reach Earth’s surface
options.C:
The mass of a muon as measured on Earth is
different from the mass measured in the rest
frame of a muon, so the acceleration due to
gravity is different.
options.D:
Energies of muons as measured on Earth
are different from the energies measured in
the rest frame of a muon, so different
interactions occur with the atmosphere.
